Event Name/Title

Gravity Of Offence

Event Date

03 Sep 1990

Event Location

India, Mumbai

Event Type

Temple - SB class

Transcriber

Sharad Keshari

Completed

03 Apr 2021

e.g.

Verse

SB 8.7.22

Tags – Places

Navadvip, Sri Lanka, Ocean of Milk, Vrindavan

Tags – Pastimes

Lord serving Sudama Vipra, story of gopal chapal, offence by yadus to the sages, Daksha offending Lord Shiva

Tags – Persons

Ambarish Maharaj, Lord Shiva, Haridas Thakur, Srivas Thakur, Krishna, Gopal Chapal, Durvasa Muni, Lord Chaitanya, Ramchandra Khan, Samba, Lord Krishna, Lord Ram, Narad Muni, Parvat Muni, Sati, Shrila Prabhupad

Tags – Themes

Offence to devotees, how much Lord loves his devotees, dealing with devotees

Lecture Transcription

tvam ekaḥ sarva-jagata
īśvaro bandha-mokṣayoḥ
taṁ tvām arcanti kuśalāḥ
prapannārti-haraṁ gurum

[ŚB 8.7.22]

Translation

O lord, you are the cause of bondage and liberation of the entire universe because you are its ruler. Those who are advanced in spiritual consciousness surrender unto you, and therefore you are the cause of mitigating their distresses, and you are also the cause of their liberation. We therefore worship Your Lordship.

Purport

Actually Lord Viṣṇu maintains and accomplishes all good fortune. If one has to take shelter of Lord Viṣṇu, why should the demigods take shelter of Lord Śiva? They did so because Lord Viṣṇu acts through Lord Śiva in the creation of the material world. Lord Śiva acts on behalf of Lord Viṣṇu. When the Lord says in Bhagavad-gītā (14.4) that He is the father of all living entities (ahaṁ bīja-pradaḥ pitā), this refers to actions performed by Lord Viṣṇu through Lord Śiva. Lord Viṣṇu is always unattached to material activities, and when material activities are to be performed, Lord Viṣṇu performs them through Lord Śiva. Lord Śiva is therefore worshiped on the level of Lord Viṣṇu. When Lord Viṣṇu is untouched by the external energy He is Lord Viṣṇu, but when He is in touch with the external energy, He appears in His feature as Lord Śiva.

The unique position of Lord Shiva

[Mangalacharan] [Maharaj reads Verse, Translation, and Purport] In this chapter of the Srimad Bhagavatam we find by the desire and guidance of Lord Sri Vishnu, the demons and the demigods together, on the pretense of great crisis in their life, are approaching Lord Shankar. And in their praise to Lord Shive, they are approaching him with the same words of praise as they would praise the Supreme Personality of Godhead Himself. Of course, we know that Lord Shiva has a very, very special and unique position. He is a gunavatar, when he appears in this material world. But beyond his manifestation in this material creation Lord Shiva is none other than the supreme Lord Vishnu, but just as explained in Brahma-samhita, when milk is touched by a culture it becomes yogurt and therefore it eternally becomes different than milk, different qualities. Although it is essentially the same substance. Similarly, when Lord Vishnu accepts the influence of the externa energy upon himself, he becomes Lord Shiva. In this way they are simultaneously one and different. The living entity is Jiva-tatva but Lord Shiva is never to be considered as Jiva-tatva. He is considered to be Shiva tatva. He is in a category of his own. He is Vishnu but has eternally manifested in such a way that he does not exhibit the same qualities as Vishnu. But Lord Shiva is considered the param Vaishnava, the supreme Vaishnava. vaiṣṇavānāṁ yathā śambhuḥ [SB 12.13.16], that there is no vaishnava who is greater than Lord Shiva. In his position he has taken the role of a devotee of Lord Narayan. And therefore, all vaishnavas of all bona fide sampradayas worship Lord Shiva as the greatest of all devotees. In Vrindavan, sometimes we see the murti of Lord Shiva sitting in his traditional pose, in the lotus position sitting in meditation or samadhi. And of course, in Vrindavan their idea is that Lord Shiva is meditating on the divine lila of Shri Shri Radha and Krishna. In this way he is constantly immersed. His mind is constantly fixed on the lotus feet of Lord Shyamsundar.

The Lord worshipping His own devotee

So, one who takes shelter of the lotus feet of the Lord becomes most dear to the Lord. And if one wants to actually give pleasure to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, one must first offer his respect and gain the blessings of such a great soul. In fact, Lord Sri Ramachandra himself, at the holy place of Rameswaram, at the time of the battle with Ravana at Sri Lanka, Lord Sri Ramachandra, he worshipped Lord Shiva. And this is the cause of bewilderment to many persons. That if Lord Ramachandra is the supreme absolute truth, the Supreme Personality of Godhead and Shiva is His devotee, then why is He worshiping as such? Because the Lord takes great pleasure in worshipping his devotee. Just as in Dwarka, when Sudama Vipra came to the home of Krishna, Krishna also worshiped this brahmana. He washed Sudama’s feet with his own lotus-like hands and Rukmini, the goddess of fortune, with the chamar, fanned him, to give him pleasure. Similarly, at Rameswaram the Supreme Personality of Godhead worshipped His devotee Lord Shiva with great, great love, to show the glory of the devotee. The first offence to the holy name is, to blaspheme the devotees, who are dedicating their lives to uplift others by propagating the holy name. The Lord Himself worships His devotee. So, what is our position, to disrespect such a great soul?

api cet su-durācāro
bhajate mām ananya-bhāk
sādhur eva sa mantavyaḥ
samyag vyavasito hi saḥ

[Bg 9.30]

[Even if one commits the most abominable action, if he is engaged in devotional service he is to be considered saintly because he is properly situated in his determination.]

Krishna says in the nineth chapter of Bhagavad-Gita that, “Even if one commits abominable activities, if such a person is my devotee, I consider him a sadhu.” So, what is your position to question and justify, blasphemy of such a person? So, this is a person engaged in abominable activities, Krishna considers him as saintly and offers him all respect. What to speak of a pure devotee, who is dedicated in body, mind, and words to the service of the lotus feet of the Lord. How exalted, how magnanimous, how worshippable are such a person’s lotus feet.

Consequences of offending someone dear to the Lord

Therefore, to even accidentally offend such a person is very, very dangerous. What to speak of willingly? Therefore, one should be very, very careful. Vaishnava aparadha is considered the mad elephant offence. That may take you, life times and life times and life times to develop your garden of bhakti and if a mad elephant comes into that garden, within a moment the whole garden is torn to pieces. So similarly, by offending the great souls, all of our spiritual progress is uprooted, at least for the time being and we see in the case of Lord Shiva. Daksha, during the sacrifice that he was performing, he disrespected Lord Shankar. How did he disrespect? He was seeing him according to material vision. arcye viṣṇau śilā-dhīr guruṣu nara-matir vaiṣṇave jāti-buddhir [Padma Purana, SB 4.21.12 purport]. It is said that one who sees the worshippable deity to be made of wood or stone, one who sees the charanamrita to be simply ordinary water, one who sees guru to be an ordinary man with material vision, or one who sees the vaishnava belong to any particular country, cast, or creed, such person is of hellish mentality. His consciousness is one of the hellish planets. So, this is the great caution that one must always be aware of, not to ever see the great souls with material vision.

Daksha offending Lord Shiva

Daksha was seeing that ‘Here is Lord Shiva’, the fact is he is always associating with ghosts, persons who are deeply drenched in the mode of ignorance. Usually you can tell a man by its company. He engages in activities that are apparently in the mode of ignorance. He smokes hashish, smokes the ganja. He also wears crematorium ashes on his body, which is an activity of worship in the mode of ignorance, tamoguna. He frequently goes to the place of crematoriums, the places which are untouchable. In the traditional Vedic system, if you are at a crematorium to see a funeral, you are not even allowed to go into the temple for so many weeks. Lord Shiva, he does his bhajans in those places. He wears serpents surround his neck. So, Daksha, who actually unfortunately, was too familiar with Lord Shiva. Familiarity breeds contempt. This is a great danger. People who become too familiar with great souls, especially the spiritual master, their tendency is to see him very ordinary, just like judging him according to material calculations and gradually commit offenses. So, Daksha happened to be the father in law of Lord Shiva. His daughter, Sati was Lord Shiva’s wife. So, Daksha therefore was seeing Lord Shiva, due to this familiarity, in a very material way. Rather than seeing the depth of his great surrender to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, he was simply seeing these external apparent contradictions in Lord Shiva’s behavior. Therefore, Daksha began to insult Lord Shiva and due to this offence, he lost everything. He lost his fame; he lost his very daughter. She could not tolerate it. This is the nature of a devotee. A devotee cannot tolerate hearing Vaishnava aparadha. Sati was so much angered and so much distressed by hearing the worshipable Lord, her guru maharaja, her husband Lord Shiva being insulted that She took her very life. She set with her own body on fire through the yogic process, because she was thinking that ‘This Daksha being my relation, I don’t want any relation to him anymore. He is my father, he has given me this body, I do not want any body, that has any relationship with him. He has polluted my ears; he has polluted my heart.’ And this is actually the seriousness that a devotee sees the vaishnava aparadha. It is said in the scriptures that if one is hearing a vaishnava being offended, one must either soundly defeat that person on argument to shut his mouth or one must take that person’s life or one must cut that person’s tongue out or one take one’s own life because his heart has been polluted by hearing this aparadha or one must immediately leave the place. So, Sati after hearing the contaminating words, decided that ‘This life was no longer fit.’ Because she had heard such offence to a devotee. So, she decided to burn it within the fire of Yoga. And then of course, Daksha, he had his head cut off and was given the head of a goat as reaction. Even though, you have to understand that Daksha was a very highly religious man. In many ways, he was a guru in his own right. He was not just an ordinary person. He was one of the prajapatis, that means he was very highly learned in the sashtra. He was performing tremendous, tremendous sacrifices exactly according to the Vedas. He was factually more religious and more spiritually minded than practically anyone today. But even such a person, in such an exulted position, due to the offence against a great soul, he was punished very severely by the mercy of Lord.

Other instances of Vaishnava aparadha

Throughout the Vedic literatures we find how intolerable the Lord sees the offences against his devotees. In Caitanya-Caritamrita there is the story of Gopal Chapal. Gopal Chapal, He offended Srivas Thakur. He was considering Srivas Thakur, after all he was a grihasta. He had children and he had wife, what so great about him? He thought him to be an ordinary man and accused him of being a worshiper of goddess Durga for material purposes. He even arranged all the Durga Puja paraphernalia one night outside of his house. He brought the town’s people and said, “You see, he is worshiping Durga.” And Srivas Thakur said, “Yes, of course! I am worshiping Durga.” Srivas Thakur was not the slightest bit concerned for his name and fame. But the next day Gopal Chapal came down with leprosy and of course, although he was a very wealthy and very religious man in his own right, he was banned from living in the community of Navadvip. He had to stay on the banks of Ganges alone as a beggar. And when Lord Caitanya one day was passing by. Gopal Chapal fell at his feet begging forgiveness, “Please I am sorry for what I have done. Please cure me from this horrible suffering disease.” And Lord Caitanya just looked at him with great anger and said, “Because you have offended My devotee, there is no salvation for you, you will suffer. Not only in this life, but for millions of births you will suffer.” Lord Caitanya just walked on. The Lord is all merciful but not to one who offends his devotee. And then sometimes later Lord Caitanya was walking by that area again. And Gopal Chapal, he was actually genuinely repentant for the offence that he had committed to the Lord’s devotee, Srivas Thakur and he begged, “Please, whatever you want to do with me, let me never offend your devotees again.” And then the Lord said, “If you get the blessings of Srivas Thakur then again you can be saved.” So, Gopal Chapal went to the feet of Srivas Thakur and with a heart of genuine sincerity, not just superficially, with genuine sincerity and gravity, he begged Srivas Thakur, “Please! forgive me.” And of course, Srivas Thakur was saying, “What have you done?” But this is the fact that he went through, that process with the sincere heart, not just as a formality but he was really repentant, the Lord cured him of his leprosy and Gopal Chapal actually became a very good devotee of Srivas Thakur and the other vaishnavas. Similarly, we read of Gopal Chakravarti. He publicly insulted Haridas Thakur and because of that insult he also attained leprosy. His fingers fell off, he lost everything, suffering a terrible condition of life. There are so many examples. In the Bhagavatam we have the story of Durvasa Muni, such a great yogi, such a great religious person. But due to his offence against Ambarish maharaja, there was no salvation anywhere, even at the feet of Vishnu. Narottam Das Thakur has prayed that, “My dear Lord Govinda, you are tolerant even against offences to Yourself.” If one offends Krishna, by chanting the holy name one can be purified, but if one offends his devotee, there is no salvation for that person. Unless that person completely humbles himself at the feet of such a great soul. And Krishna, how much could he emphasize it? His own family, the Yadu Dynasty. He arranged for them to leave the planet as a reaction of an offence against the devotees. Some of the members of the Yadu Dynasty, they dressed Samba up as a pregnant woman because they wanted to ridicule some of the great sages Narada Muni, Parvat Muni, and some others. And they brought Samba before Narad Muni, the others. They said, “O great sages, you are seers of past, present, and future. Tell us, will this young lady give birth to a boy or a girl?” And they were thinking, whatever they said, then they will just reveal that he was really Samba and just start laughing at these great sages. Thinking ‘We are Kshatriyas and are very powerful than these brahmanas.’ They were seeing them in an ordinary way. Narada Muni understood that mentality was offensive against vaishnavas. So Narada muni said, “You will give birth to a lump of iron that will be the cause of the destruction of the entire Yadu Dynasty.” And they became very much afraid and then right then and there from Samba’s stomach came a lump of Iron. So Prabhupada explains in the first canto, in a purport that this is the gravity of an offense against a devotee. That Krishna showed, that even if you are his own family members, the greatest people on earth, the Yadu Dynasty, the whole dynasty will be destroyed if you offend a devotee. In the story of Ramchandra Khan, due to his offense against Srila Haridas Thakur, not only he was put in prison, all his wealth was plundered, his whole family members were put in the prison and whole village and city that he was living in was completely devastated. It is explained that the offence against a devotee is so serious that not only you but your family and your whole society around you has to pay the consequences.

Pleasing the Lord by pleasing His devotees

So, we are giving these examples just to show how dear the devotee is to the Lord. And just as the Lord accepts so much offence, when we offend his devotees, you can imagine how much pleasure he gains when you praise His devotees, when you glorify his devotees. Haa... mad-bhakta-pūjābhyadhikā [SB 11.19-20-24]. Krishna told Uddhava that, “One who calls himself my devotee is not my devotee. But one who calls himself a devotee of my devotee, he is my most beloved devotee”. yasya prasādād bhagavat-prasādo yasyāprasādān na gatiḥ kuto ’pi [Guruashtaka Verse 8]. One who attains the mercy of guru gets the mercy of Krishna. One who cannot get the mercy of guru, he cannot get the mercy of Krishna, no matter what else he does. Krishna arranges so that he is not approached directly. He is approached through the grace of the great souls, the sadhus. So, Lord Shiva is the representing here, the supreme spiritual master, the great vaishnava and the glory of Lord Shiva, that makes him so infinitely dear to Vishnu, is about to be shown through this most wonderful pastime of the Srimad Bhagavatam. In order to show compassion to the fallen souls, who are in great distress, Lord Shiva is about to drink the halahala, the great poison which has origin from the ocean of milk. He is about to risk his life and accept a very torturous suffering condition. It is described that there is no superior service that one can offer to Lord Krishna than the willingness to suffer, pain, and to risk one’s life to up lift others. Because Krishna is the father and he loves all of his children and one who is willing to take great risk to rescue one of his children becomes the dear most to his heart. Just like, even in this ordinary world, if a child is in great danger and somebody risks his life and even endures great, great pain and suffering to save the child, how dear that person would be to the father and mother. There would no person more dear in the world. So similarly, this is the position of Krishna, that he loves each and every one of his parts and parcels infinitely. And one who is willing, even at the cost of imminent death or pain to uplift even one of such persons becomes the dear most to the Lord. And therefore, Krishna says that, “One who instructs this transcendental knowledge to others, is most dear to me and there will never be anyone more dear to me”. So, to glorifying such a person is the supreme glorification of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And of course, to glorify such a person means to glorify from one’s heart, to glorify through one’s activities through one’s life. Is there any questions? Yes?

Q&A Transcription

[Devotee:] [00:23:47 – 00:24:04 inaudible and unclear]

[Maharaj:] In the purport Prabhupada explains like this. But that is not the subject of what we are discussing in class today. Is there any other questions?

[Devotee:] [00:24:15 – 00:24:22 inaudible and unclear]

[Maharaj:] Its canto eight, chapter seven.

[Devotee:] [00:24:23 – 00:24:50 inaudible and unclear]

[Maharaj:] manuṣyāṇāṁ sahasreṣu kaścid yatati siddhaye [Bg 7.3]. Out of many persons, one even endeavors through this knowledge. And of those who endeavor, even hardly one knows him in truth. It takes a very, very sincere soul to leave his pride behind and become the humble servant of the servant. The tendency is that we have any inclination to not let go our material pride. Even in very high spiritual positions we can become envious of someone who is doing great work. In the material world we do not like anyone to do things that excel us. And in spiritual circles, even though you may become quite spiritually advanced on one level, if you do not give up your pride, you will think like this. It will be intolerable for you to see someone else advancing or being more empowered than you. And therefore, you try to put yourself first by tearing that person down. So, we have to be very much on guard of this enemy of pride. It is the last of the enemies to leave us. Ahankar, false ego. To give up lust, to give up anger, to give up greed is very difficult. But after all these things are given up, still pride is very, very powerful force within your heart. That’s the most painful and difficult of all the anarthas to give up.

[Devotee:] [00:26:33 – 00:26:40 inaudible and unclear]

[Maharaj:] Hmm.

[Devotee:] [00:26:41 – 00:26:44 inaudible and unclear]

[Maharaj:] He did not offend him. If you read the Bhagavatam, you find that, that was Krishna’s Yoga maya. It was not due to envy. Krishna put him in a situation, where it wasn’t even hardly an offense. Put him in a situation because he wanted the Bhagavatam to be spoken. That was Krishna’s arrangement that had nothing to do with pride or envy. He is simply being used as a puppet in Krishna’s hands. So that the Srimad Bhagavatam would be spoken. Hare Krishna.
